Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd sit down and work through the readings, but then I'd get distracted or something else would come up. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually late at night when the anxiety about falling behind got too intense. I'd spend hours reading through materials but never in any organized way.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I work part-time and have other responsibilities that eat into my schedule, so I couldn't really commit to regular study times. Some weeks I'd barely look at course stuff, then I'd have a panic moment and try to cram everything at once. Deadlines didn't even help much because by the time I noticed them, it was often too late.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or sudden panic that made me log in. I'd realize I hadn't checked the course in a while and feel this wave of anxiety about what I might have missed. Sometimes I'd log in just to reassure myself that I wasn't completely lost, even if I didn't actually do anything productive once I was there. Notifications from classmates posting in discussions would sometimes prompt me too.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to the point where logging in felt automatic or normal. It always felt like this thing I should be doing but wasn't. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a bit with the anxiety, but they didn't translate into regular LMS habits for me. I'd go through phases where I'd try to make it routine, but it never stuck.
Q5: The written materials and reading resources were most useful to me since I learn better by reading and writing things out. When I actually engaged with them, I could take notes in my own way and process the information at my own pace. The discussion posts were interesting too because seeing how other students interpreted things sometimes clicked for me in ways the lectures didn't.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the video content. Sitting through videos felt like a huge time commitment and I'd zone out constantly. I also barely touched the assignments until it was way too late, which is embarrassing because when I finally did one, I actually did well on it. I kept putting them off because the anxiety made starting feel impossible.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate relevance or when it wasn't overwhelming to start. Short, clearly defined tasks were easier to convince myself to do. If something required multiple steps or felt vague about what was expected, I'd just close the tab and tell myself I'd come back to it later. I rarely did.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they didn't exist to me. With my anxiety already making required work feel impossible, optional stuff was completely off my radar. Required activities at least had this external pressure that sometimes pushed me to act, even if I usually acted too late. Optional things just became invisible.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them with a notebook away from my computer. Being online made me anxious and distracted, so I preferred having physical notes I could flip through. I also used some external websites and YouTube videos to understand concepts the course materials didn't explain well enough for me.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely trying to learn but it wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd spend hours with downloaded readings, writing out summaries and trying to understand concepts. Or I'd be working through problems in a notebook, teaching myself stuff that I'd missed from not attending. None of that would register in any system.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a PDF or document once, then work with it for days or weeks without going back to the LMS. It was easier to have everything saved locally where I could access it without internet issues or the stress of being on the platform. My low resources meant I couldn't always rely on consistent internet anyway.
Q12: The hardest moments were when my anxiety peaked and everything felt overwhelming. I'd look at how much I'd missed and just freeze completely. There were weeks where I couldn't bring myself to even check what was due because I knew it would spiral me into panic. Work demands and financial stress made it worse because I couldn't prioritize school the way I needed to.
Q13: Absolutely. When work got busy or I was exhausted, the LMS was the first thing to go. Confusion about assignments made me avoid them entirely rather than ask for help. The fatigue was real too - being 28 and juggling everything, sometimes I just didn't have the mental energy left for coursework after handling life responsibilities.
Q14: All the time. I'd scroll through materials, technically accessing them, but nothing was sinking in. My mind would be racing with anxiety about everything I needed to do, and I'd read the same paragraph five times without absorbing it. It was like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: I postponed almost everything important. The bigger the assignment, the more I avoided it. I knew it was self-sabotage but the anxiety made starting feel physically impossible. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace first, and that headspace never came. By the time I forced myself to start, deadlines had usually passed.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense bursts of panic-driven activity. I'd suddenly log in multiple times trying to figure out what I needed to submit. But honestly, most deadlines passed me by because I'd avoided checking until it was too late. The deadline pressure that works for some people just paralyzed me more.
Q17: I barely got feedback because I barely submitted anything. The one assignment I did complete, I scored really well on, which was actually surprising and made me feel worse about all the ones I'd missed. That positive feedback didn't motivate me to do more - it just highlighted how badly I'd messed up the rest.
Q18: The course structure felt rigid in ways that didn't work for me. Having eight online courses meant everything blurred together and I couldn't keep track of what was due where. The structure assumed consistent engagement that I couldn't maintain with my schedule and mental state. I needed more flexibility than was built in.
Q19: The sheer volume of content made engagement harder. When I'd log in after missing time, seeing everything piled up was paralyzing. Clearer, smaller chunks might have helped me feel like I could actually complete something. The discussion requirements were manageable though - I could contribute there without the pressure of formal assignments.
Q20: That sounds like me honestly. I'd log in, click around, read things, participate in discussions, but then freeze when it came to actual submissions. There's a huge gap between engaging with content and producing formal work. The anxiety of being evaluated, of submitting something that proves I don't understand - that's what stopped me from converting activity into assignments.
Q21: I can see that happening for students who are efficient or study offline like I tried to. If someone downloads everything and studies independently, their low LMS activity wouldn't reflect their actual learning. I wish that was my story, but my low activity matched my poor performance. I was learning some things offline, just not enough.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're present and clicking around, but they can't show whether anything is actually registering in your brain. They miss all the offline work, the anxiety spirals, the reading and rereading that happens away from the screen. They definitely can't capture how overwhelmed someone feels or why they're avoiding things.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through motions. It's when you're curious, when you want to understand rather than just pass. For me, I had moments of genuine engagement when I was working through readings offline, but the formal course structure never captured that. Real engagement is internal, not measurable by clicks.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I actually wanted to succeed despite my failures. It can't show the anxiety that made me avoid logging in, or the hours I spent with downloaded materials trying to catch up. It doesn't capture that I'm 28, working, struggling financially, and dealing with mental health stuff that makes this all harder than it looks. My data looks like I didn't care, but I cared too much - that was part of the problem.
